column9: Marker Description
Used to help detect, diagnose, and evaluate the severity of congestive heart failure. Peptide released in response to cardiac wall stress or stretch.
Marker Description

Acute phase inflammatory reactant made in the liver.  It is primarily synthesized by hepatocytes under stimulation from TNF, IL-1, and IL-6. Blood plasma is oxidizing.  Early marker for cellular inflammation.  Involved with Leptin Resistance.  Mitochondrial Leakage at cytochrome 1.
Byproduct of methionine production, Methylation status marker.  Inflammatory if high.
Breakdown product of purine protein to be removed during excretion.
Breakdown product of purine protein to be removed during excretion.
Marker Description

Measures the circulating Iron in the blood that is attached to transferrin.  Makes up some protein (like hemoglobin) and is involved in oxygen transport, cellular respiration, removal of carbon dioxide and toxin residue from cells.
About 1/3rd of your blood’s transferrin is always working to transport iron.  That leaves extra iron-binding capacity known as UIBC.
Measures the body’s ability to bind iron with transferrin.
It compares the amount of iron in the blood stream to the capacity of the red blood cells to transport iron.
It compares the amount of iron in the blood to the capacity of the blood to transport iron in percentage.
Key role in normal functioning of brain, nervous system and red blood cell formation and blood oxygenation.
Vitamin B9, key player in Methylation and prevention of cancers and cognitive disorders. Formation of red blood cells and DNA.
The most sensitive test to detect iron deficiency. Main storage form of iron in the body.
The most sensitive test to detect iron deficiency. Main storage form of iron in the body.

Are major building blocks of very low density lipoproteins (VLDL) and play an important role in metabolism as energy sources and transporters of dietary fat.
The "good" cholesterol, it carries cholesterol away from your arteries to your liver.
Triglyceride / HDL (TG / HDL) ratio is a calculated measure that can help assess your risk of heart disease and metabolic syndrome.
The "bad” cholesterol, responsible for plaque build-up in the arteries.  stabilizes the inner mitochondrial membrane during heavy cellular energy production = more ATP.

Small dense LDL, stays in circulation longer, more easily oxidized and can invade arterial walls easier.
Mediates reverse cholesterol transport by returning excess cholesterol from peripheral tissue to the liver.
Considered a direct measure of atherogenic lipoproteins.
Most powerful genetic risk factor for cardiovascular disease.

Enzyme chiefly found in the brain, skeletal muscles, and heart. Functions in cellular energy metabolism.  Part of the ATP creatine system (fastest way to make ATP) without oxygen but is dependent on methylation.  Used up in first burst of exercise.
Marker Description
The body’s chief source of energy. It affects all organs, systems and tissues.  It helps determine the acid/alkaline balance (pH).
Hormones from the pancreas that acts to take sugar out the blood stream and enter it into cells for energy production.
Measures blood glucose that has attached itself to hemoglobin.  This test more accurately measures glucose levels over the two-three weeks prior to the blood test.
Regulates glucose and fatty acid breakdown.
Hormone secreted from fat and nerve cells to signal to the brain satiety.  Involved in keeping energy balances between the brain and body.
Helps your skin tan, Increase endorphins, Needed for gut integrity, kills staph and candida, Insulin sensitivity, collagen production, needed for thyroid function.
Marker Description

About bone metabolism: the most important element in the body.  Maintains cardiac regularity & is required for muscle relaxation & contraction; necessary for enzyme production; growth and development of teeth, bones, & resistance to infection.

Albumin is a major constituent of blood proteins. It is produced mainly in the liver, where it plays a major role in distributing water and serving as a transport protein for hormones and various drugs. It is also responsible for about 80% of fluid pressure and blood vessel condition.
Secreted by the parathyroid and involved in bone metabolism and calcium homeostasis.

Use to diagnose vitamin D insufficiency and proxy for UV light expsosure. 
Controls the level of phosphate and calcium in blood, Regulates bone health,  Invigorates the immune system,  Regulates neuromuscular functions, Plays a vital role in cardiovascular functions,  Balances blood sugar level and insulin production, Promotes normal cellular growth.

Important for many different enzymatic reactions such as carbohydrate metabolism, protein synthesis, nucleic acid synthesis, muscular contraction, oxidative phosphorylation, ATP production, and is used by the body in the blood clotting mechanism. Critical to smooth muscle function, including heart, gastrointestinal tract and uterus; helps regulate acid-alkaline (base) balance in the body. Aids in absorption and metabolism of minerals such as calcium, phosphorus, sodium, and potassium; also utilization of vitamin B complex, C, and E. Regulates body temperature.
Marker Description

Sodium constitutes 90% of the electrolyte fluid facilitating cellular transport. It also acts as the main base of the blood and functions in maintaining osmotic pressure, aiding in nerve impulse transmission, as well as renal, cardiac, and adrenal functions. Essential to acid-base balance and intra/extra cellular fluid exchanges for normal body water

Essential to heart & kidney function and the maintenance of pH of both blood & urine. It maintains regular heart rate and muscle force, thus helps to prevents heart and general muscle fatigue.  Excreted by the kidneys daily.
Indicates kidney, bladder, and bowel function.  Essential for electrolyte balance and pH maintenance.
Bicarbonate is a vital component of controlling the pH of the body.  Byproduct of mitochondria energy production using oxygen.

Reflects the ratio between the production and clearance of urea. Urea, which is formed in the liver, is the final product in protein catabolism. Reveals the degree of toxicity of protein to the kidneys.
Necessary for tissue function, growth, & repair. Fluid balance, protection against infection.  Key building block of the human body.
A waste product produced by muscles, which is removed by the kidneys.  Also related to the methylation system.
Biomarker of kidney function.
Used to screen for and detect early kidney damage, to help diagnose kidney disease, and monitor kidney status.
Used to screen for and detect early kidney damage, to help diagnose kidney disease, and monitor kidney status.
Relates to kidney removal of water, protein, and tissue residue.  Mitochondrial production of EZ water.

An enzyme associated with the liver, heart, and skeletal muscle.
Relates to liver enzyme activity, kidney & skeletal muscle.
Liver damage, biliary obstruction of bile ducts.  Low antioxidant levels, Accurate predictor of mortality.  More dangerous if combined with high ferritin.

Refers to a group of isoenzymes that are found in the bone, liver, intestines, skin, and placenta.  Indicates how the liver is utilizing protein and fats, and pH balance.  (an enzyme found essentially in bone & liver.)
Bilirubin is the end product of hemoglobin breakdown from red blood cells.
The most sensitive test for liver function/dysfunction.

A pituitary gland hormone that stimulates the thyroid gland to secrete additional T4 and some T3
Reflects the total output of the thyroid gland and actual T4 hormone released.
The measure of active T4 in the blood but, must be converted to T3 to impact metabolism.
T3 is the most active thyroid hormone which is largely protein–bound but not necessarily available for metabolic activity
This test measures the free or active T3 hormone (unbound) levels, which is the actual hormones that culminates in an increase in metabolism and energy.
Check in cases of autoimmune thyroid disorders.
Check in cases of autoimmune thyroid disorders.  This antibody is associated with thyroid cancer.
